By the way, before I forget, there's something I would like you to do. You might want to take some notes here, okay? Not everybody, just him. The, you test for a hundred and two different types of allergies. Okay? If you haven't done this already, rank those allergies. Like if you do a thousand tests, how many, you know, allergies to bananas showed up. Show those in rank order and then do them, kick them out by blood type. Kick them out by race, kick them out by age, and kick them out by every other segment that you can think of. I'll tell you an interesting little story that will illustrate where I'm going with that thought. There was an advertising agency in New York that didn't know how to do advertising, so they hired me under cover to come up and do it, which has happened more often than you would think. And they were selling a product invented by some guy who supposedly was an automotive expert. And what this product did was it increased the gas mileage of a car, at least that's what it's supposed to have done. And so they ran ads for it in very general media like Parade Publications, which is a newspaper distributed magazine which is read by millions of people. Very homogenous audience, non-targeted audience. And they got an awful lot of response. And they fought me on this, but I prevailed. What I finally got them to do is to rank the response by make, year, and model of cars. Then I had them contact the automobile manufacturers and find out how many cars were manufactured of each make, year, and model. And then we did a very simple mathematical correlation. And you could tell what percentage of Chevrolet and Pala owners were bought this as opposed to what percentage of Cadillac civil owned the product. And as you might expect, there was a dramatic difference. As I recall, it was like a three or four year old Lincoln was the top car that people owned where they wanted to buy this. And I think the Volkswagen and the Volvo's, the gas misers, were at the bottom. And so what we did is we got in touch with Reuben Donnelly Corporation who maintains this monstrous mailing list which is overlaid with all kinds of demographic information. And we had them kick out the names in rank order by car. So we would start with three year old Lincoln owners. And I wrote a letter and it said something like, Dear friend, in such and such a year, 140,723 Lincoln town car X107s were manufactured. As of now, 72,109 of them are still on the road. The reason I'm writing this letter is because I have something important to say to everyone who still has this car. And the big headline, you are all being cheated. Then I went right into the spiel about that. Now the point of that is, is I had identified a mailing list and that's another way to make a compiled list work. And you don't know if there will be those kinds of differences until you do the research. And you may find out that there is a big difference between black people and Caucasians and Asians and Latinos. You may find a difference in age groups. You may find a difference in blood types. It would be real interesting to know. Okay. What would you all like me to talk about this one? Huh? I mean about anything in particular. You were writing that letter before you got it. You had some kind of a structure in your mind just to get the idea of flow. You got the pre down the head and the sub head in your first line. But then as you went through the letter as I was writing it, I was thinking there was a problem statement, a problem solution, evidence, the door is closed, and I couldn't make out exactly what it was. I wonder if you could let us know. Nah. How about still reading up the first letter? Right. Nah. How about I show you how it's done in the real world? Sam and Leslie Fishbine own Casey Fine Furniture in Denver, Colorado. They have five scores. Sam was a client of mine years ago. And I met him in Denver on a social visit a few months ago, and he told me that the work I did for them then is still the most profitable thing that they do. They didn't have five scores that they could do now. And so then he came to my seminar the last of our two months ago. And then he hired me. And the way he and I work is that he sits at a computer with a 20-inch screen, and he and I sit there in brainstorm, and he goes tippy tippy tappin', one category goes the I.G. is another category, scores the finished copy, et cetera, et cetera. And by the end of the telephone conversation, generally, we have one or more ads or direct mail letters totally crafted out. He adds, you know, there's a point where we do the yada yada yada yada. I say, okay, the yada yada goes here, and the blah blah blah goes here, and he says, yeah, yeah, yeah, we got that, et cetera, et cetera. And basically what Sam has to sell and his benefits are the same all the time. We have the same message to present all the time. And here is his deal when you buy furniture. One, you don't have to make any down payment whatsoever. Two, you don't have to make any payments at all for an entire year. Three, you will not have to pay a single penny in interest. Four, you get to take a look at some of the most deeply discounted furniture in our 31-year history. Five, you get your choice of everything you want for your home with fast in-stock or factory delivery. Now, that's the message, and that's a very interesting message to you if you're in the market for furniture. If you're not in the market for furniture, it won't be of any interest at all. Now, what I told Sam when we began, since we have the same thing to say over and over, there are two things that we have got to do to make his ads work really well. Number one is we have to do something that involves theater, showmanship, and drama to call people's attention to this message. People who buy furniture want a sale. That's the end of the story. They want a sale. So the second part of it is that we have to come up with a reason why for the sale. So most of our brainstorming sessions consist of ways to dramatize and add showmanship to his advertising and ways to, and reason why that you should come in now. Okay, this is the first one. We did it in the first session. This is one I'm going to read the entire ad to you. The rest of them I will not because it won't be necessary. Free video reveals how to get all the new furniture you need, even if you don't have any money. My name is Leslie Fishbine and I am in trouble. I am the owner of Casey Fine Furniture here in Denver. And friends, we have five stores closed for friends. And I now have a problem that is very unusual. As you know, the East Coast has been periodically paralyzed by massive amounts of snow this winter. This has caused hundreds of furniture stores in New York, New Jersey, and other eastern states to almost go out of business. Let's face it, if your customers can't get to your store, you can't sell any furniture or anything else. And even though this happened back east, it has had a major effect on my business here in Denver. You see, all those stores back east had to cancel almost all their orders for new furniture from the factories in North Carolina, where most of the furniture in the U.S. is manufactured. These manufacturers now have their warehouses filled to the rafters with furniture waiting for final production. Two of our best suppliers called me yesterday and offered to practically give away $783,000 worth of their unsold production, if I could pay them immediately with cold hard cash. According to them, they've got to do something right away or else start laying off many of their workers. They hate the idea of doing this because some of those people have been with them for as long as 30 years. Anyway, the bargains they offered me were so incredible, I immediately said yes and sent them a certified check for all the extra money I had available in my business checking account. So guess what? Now, it is now me who has the cash flow problem. However, I think I know how to solve this problem. Look, I'm putting all this furniture I just purchased, Perence, stole is more like it, close Perence. From those desperate manufacturers in North Carolina on sale at incredible prices, however, this once in a lifetime opportunity is only going to last for 10 hours and 15 minutes. That will happen at my downtown store at 1201 O'Rourya Parkway this Thursday on April 4th from 10.45 in the morning until exactly 9 p.m. in the evening. I'm practically giving away my inventory for this sale. Here's what I can offer you for one day only this coming Thursday on the 4th of the month. And then we go through it. One, you don't have to make any down payment whatsoever. Two, you don't have to make any payments that offer an entire year. Three, you will not have to pay a single penny in interest. Four, you get to take a look at some of the most deeply discounted furniture in our 31-year history. Five, you get your choice of everything you want for your home with fast-in stock or factory delivery. Sounds like I'm crazy, doesn't it? Well, I'm not. All I am is a very motivated woman who has been forced to create the biggest furniture sale in my life because I have no other choice. Look, if you, A, want new furniture without waiting or B, want your furniture custom made or C, if you want furniture now but you don't want to pay for it for a long time, or D, you simply want to get in on sale of a lifetime, you need to come to my downtown store this Thursday without fail. Remember, this may be the only chance you ever get for a deal like this and it will only last 10 hours and 15 minutes. After that, it's history. Now, get this. I have created a homemade video that shows off this furniture and if you will call my office right now, one of my assistants will send you a free copy of this video by overnight mail. This will allow you to have a visual preview of all this furniture right in the privacy of your own home. But don't wait. You better call now and tell us where to send the video or you might get left out. Call 303-778-6400. That's a full-page ad, tabloid size in the Rocky Mountain News. There are two papers in Denver, Colorado. The Denver Post and the Rocky Mountain News. The Rocky Mountain News works very well for everybody. The Denver Post doesn't work well for anybody. And who knows why? Um. Okay. Here's another letter, and I won't read the whole thing to you. This is personalized, and it says, Dear Mrs. Everett, as you can see there is a card attached to the top of this letter that has your name on it. Also, right next to your name is a seven-digit number, which has been assigned to you. Look, you may not know it, but your name and number assigned to you on that card can save you thousands of dollars. Here's why. If you will rip that card off the top of this letter and turn it over, you will see that my name is Mary Jo Kopeckney. And that's Sam and I just do this stuff. And I am an interior decorator licensed to practice here in the state of Colorado. Do you know what that means? The answer is simple. It means I can buy new furniture at the lowest price as humanly possible. Nobody can buy furniture in Denver cheaper than me. Not a contractor, not a home builder, not an apartment owner, no one. In fact, I can buy furniture cheaper than a lot of people who own furniture stores. How would you like to be able to buy all the furniture you need at the same deep, deep discounts I can get? Guess what? Now you can, but you can only do it for just one day. Here's the story. Two of my dearest friends in all the world are Sam and Leslie Fishbine. They own Casey Fine Furniture, which has five retail stores in Denver. Did you notice that word retail? To me, that's the ugliest word in the English language. I hate to pay retail. And fortunately, when it comes to furniture, I don't have to. I was talking to Sam and Leslie about this, and I asked them this question. Why don't you give some of your customers the same discount you give me? We can't explain, Leslie. Denver is so competitive that if we already have deep discounts on everything we offer, so is so competitive we already have deep discounts on everything we offer to the public. If we gave our customers the same extra discount we'd give you, we'd be selling our furniture for such low prices we might go broke. But then I explained something to them. Look, I said, look, Sam and Leslie, if you let a few of your customers buy furniture for just one day at the same low prices you give to me, I bet those people will be so excited and so grateful they will continue to do business with you for years. Well, it took quite a bit of talking on my part, but I finally got Sam and Leslie to agree to a very interesting experiment, and you are a part of that experiment. Here's the deal. Sam and Leslie have authorized me to give one of my business cards to you and a few other carefully selected homeowners here in Denver. What you need to do is this. Bring my business card to any KC Fine of KC Fine Furnitures five different locations on Wednesday, April 10. That's just a few days from now. And show it to the woman in the red jacket who will be sitting at a desk just inside the door. She will then write your name and special discount authorization code number that has been assigned to you and the ledger that she will have on the desk in front of her. After that she will use a rubber stamp, put a stamp on your wrist, which will give you access to a very special part of the store. Don't worry about that stamp on your wrist making you look funny. It won't run on your clothes and it is invisible except when you shine a special fluorescent light on it. Okay, as soon as you have your stamp, go directly to that part of the store where they have all the furniture that they will be offering for just this one day. At the same huge discount they would give to me, but on this special day, you are also going to get other special considerations. For example, yada, yada, yada, yada, yada. And that's when we do it. And that's the second one. Third one. Inside address dated so and so. Good Mrs. Everett, as you can see, but my firm's letterhead, I am attorney licensed to practice law in the state of Colorado. Just in case you want to check, my license number is 10432X and it was given to me on February 12, 1976, and is recorded in the Denver County Courthouse. I graduated from Harvard Law School in 1974 and I've been practicing both in Arizona and here in Colorado for the past 20 years. However, I'd like to put your mind at ease. The truth is, most of the time when you get a letter from an attorney at law, it is bad news. That is not the case with this letter. In fact, as far as you are concerned, this letter contains nothing but good news. Here's what has happened. I have a client here in Denver who is in a little bit of trouble. Her name is Leslie Fishbine and according to the U.S. taxpayer code, number 14036.7, section 91287B paragraphs 11 through 17. Mrs. Fishbine is hereby mandated to pay all her corporate income in federal right to occupy space on the planet, tax surcharges in a timely fashion on or before April 15th next. What this means is Mrs. Fishbine, in order to meet these obligations, must generate a substantial amount of revenues in a very short time. To do this, she has authorized me to notify various Denver homeowners who meet certain exacting criteria to purchase all the new furniture said homeowner may need at discounts that are, in some cases, as much as 90% off the suggested retail price. Take advantage of this situation. You must appear in person at 1201 Aurora Parkway on Saturday, April 13th, between the hours of 10 a.m. and 3.30 p.m. You must bring this letter with you and your driver's license in one other form of identification, such as your Social Security card, any major credit card, a City of Denver library card, a union card, or something that verifies the information on your driver's license. You must also have something that gives proof of residence. This can be a utility bill, such as your water or electric bill, a copy of your last phone statement, or any envelope or letter which has been mailed to you by any local or federal government agency which has been sent to you at your current address. Why should you bother bringing this letter in two forms of identification with you to a furniture store on April 13th, 1996? The answer is simple. Do you actually get there in this journey? Absolutely. Absolutely. So, the spread of the result is good. Here's the fastest growing furniture chain in the United States. This one's public apology. John Q. Everett, 123 Elm Street, Denver, Colorado, 80209 from Leslie Fishmine, 900 South Santa Fe Drive, Denver, Colorado, 80223. Message. Dear Mr. Everett, this letter is my public apology to you and certain other valued customers who I seem to have offended. None of this was deliberate on my part. Last month on the 30th of March, I had a private furniture sale with incredible bargains on almost everything in our $8 million inventory. It was my intention to notify and allow all of my preferred customers to attend and take advantage of this unique opportunity to buy furniture at some of the lowest prices in our 31-year history. However, what happened was so many people showed up between 8 a.m. and 9 a.m., the first hour we were open that our store was so flooded with people we had to lock the doors. Naturally, the people outside who couldn't get in became somewhat angry with us. You see, word had gotten around about what we were doing. Once folks found out about it, they all wanted to get in on those deals. I guess I can't blame them when you consider that we made it possible for the people who were able to get in the store to get all the furniture they needed. Yada, yada, yada, yada, yada. Well, it's no wonder those people who couldn't get to the store on time or couldn't get in since they did get to the store got a little angry. The only business that's the worst thing I can have is unhappy customers. I've decided to have the same identical sale one more time. This time the offer is going to be private and if you don't get in on this one, it will be your fault, not mine. I'm sending this letter by first-class mail to make sure you get it on time. Not only that, a very limited number of my most valued customers, only a limited number are getting this notice and this time I guarantee you will get in. There are a few other things you should know and this comes under yada, yada, but it's a little change. You'll save at least 33% and up to 50% off our retail list prices on every sofa, love seat, chair, ottoman section on recliner. And you'll save 40-50% off our retail list prices on everything you want for your bedroom, dining room, home office, and family room. Plus, bring this letter with you when you come in and use your good credit to get all the furniture you want with no money down, no payment at all for the entire year, or use your cash and we'll deduct an additional 10% off your entire furniture purchase. Okay, now there's just one more thing. To make up for any inconvenience I may have inadvertently caused any of my customers. This free rug is really neat. Here's a really neat clip art eagle. And then official notice a pending mail. And then there's a little box up here. Now put on my broken glasses to see what the box says. Legal notice. This letter is being sent via first class mail. Under U.S. federal law, it is illegal and felonious for anyone other than the intended recipient to open, read, alter, destroy, or tamper with this message in any way, shape, or form. Violators may be prosecuted to the full extent of the law and liable for all subsequent court charges and attorney fees and face possible incarceration. I wrote that. That sounds pretty neat, doesn't it? Okay. Now, then you open it up and we get the eagle again on yellow letter. It says notice of pending mail. I think it sort of would be like impending now. Notice of pending mail. John Q. Everett, 123 Elm Street, Denver, Colorado, 802-09. I always write to John Q. Everett. This is Mrs. John Q. Everett, actually. Dear Mrs. Everett, two weeks from now you're going to receive a very special letter. This letter will be postmarked April 17, 1996 and sent to you via first class mail. This letter will notify you of the most private discount furniture sale ever to be held within the city limits of Denver, Colorado. If you come to our store on the secret date, which will be revealed to you in this special forthcoming letter, colon A, you will be able to purchase all the furniture you need with no pay. Yada, yada, yada, yada. Okay? The letter you are going to receive, remember, it will be postmarked April 17, 1996, will be the only notice you will receive and you must bring it with you to take advantage of this very private, not open to the public discount furniture sale. There will be no notice whatsoever of this sale in the TV, radio, or newspaper media. This sale is by invitation on, make sure you read all details the same day you receive the letter. You will never get another chance like this. Important. This letter will be sent to you in a plain, unmarked, yellow envelope, very similar to the envelope used to mail this letter. Make sure you look for it on or about the 18th or 19th of this month and make sure you read it immediately. Sincerely, Leslie Fischbein. P.S., there will be a very unusual photograph included with the pending letter you are about to receive. I sincerely hope this personal photograph does not offend you. That's the right question. Now, you got this letter beyond your mind, wouldn't it? It's speaking of envelopes. Does the envelope influence sales response at all? I'm asking questions specifically, just wait. Does the outside of the envelope influence sales response at all? If you read the postcard, the type, what you just said, what's on the front of the envelope, the little evil in response. Yeah, it does. The most safest thing is what I normally do is I just use plain envelopes, but once in a while I like to do something like this. This thing has priority type, like fake FedEx envelopes, and those work pretty well. Let me show you what, then they got their priority mail. Jane Q. Everett, 123 Alps Street, Denver, Colorado, 80209, Dear Mrs. Everett, this letter contains the information promised to you in my earlier letter dated April 11th, 1996. This is your official non-transferable invitation to the most private furniture sale, discount furniture sale ever to be held within the city limits of Denver, Colorado. This invitation entitles you to the following considerations. What? Say it. Yada, yada, yada, yada. Right? Important. This letter will be the only notice you will receive, and you must bring it with you to take advantage of this very private, not open to the public discount furniture sale. This sale is by invitation only, and the general public is not invited to attend. There will be no mention whatsoever of this private, very private discount furniture sale in the TV, radio, or newspaper media. This sale is for one day only, between the hours of 8 of 10 a.m. to 9 p.m. and will be held at our 1201 Aurora Parkway Store. See and close photograph on the following date. This coming Saturday, April 27th, please be there. You will never get another chance like this in your entire life. Sincerely Leslie Fishbine. Yes, certain selected items are actually discounted at 75% below wholesale. Now, even if the question is not asked, somebody out there whose mental fecal material is especially dense packed is thinking, but there was nothing offensive about that photograph, you know? Now, what happened is they got a yellow letter with the notice of pending mail, and then they got a mention of a photograph, and then they got another letter, and all that letter said about photographs, I hope it didn't offend you. Now, those of you who want to ask that question but fortunately did not so that I don't have to humiliate you here, did you actually think somebody was going to call up and say, I was promised an offensive photograph and I didn't get one? So, well, I'm glad you asked that question because it brings up one of the most important things I have to teach. The question, what work best newspaper or direct mail? Sam has five furniture stores, and we flood those stores. We've made his wife a local celebrity. She's famous and then people ask for her autographs. We use newspapers. We use television, radio, and direct mail. And you know one of the reasons that what Sam does that works better than what other furniture dealers do in direct mail? You know why? Because he does something. He just does something. That is the biggest reason that people don't make it. It's because they don't do anything. You know, if I come back to the point, if we would just send letters that said, hi, my name is Leslie Fishbine. I own a furniture store. We got some great bargains. Why don't you come in and take a look at them? It would be better than what the other furniture dealers do in direct mail. You know why? Because they do nothing. So, we use them all. In Sam's case, he has to use them all. To answer that question yet another way, however, it's my belief that it is my belief that if you only use them, that if you only have one choice about how you're going to market, that that should be direct mail. I'll tell you, I want to explain that a little bit, and that doesn't mean that you shouldn't use the others. The reason is that direct mail has all re- I'll try to put to you another way. I used to give this example. Suppose you were in a contest with someone else on who can sell the most hamburgers. You have a hamburger stand, he has a hamburger stand. And I've asked many people, I said, what advantages would, if I could grant you any advantage you wanted, what would it be? And someone will always say, the best meat for burgers. And someone else will say, the tastiest buns. And someone else will say, the lowest prices. And someone else will say, crispy french fries to serve on the side. And so on and so on and so on. And what I like to tell people is, I'll give you all those advantages and any others that you want. If you just give me one advantage. And the advantage I want is a starving crowd. I just want my burgers in the middle of a bunch of people who are starving. Where you are most likely to fail, and I'll pound it home over and over, is there people do not want what you are offering, or not enough people who are reachable want that, or they don't want it enough. And you know, curiously, and something I learned from Eric Weinstein years ago, is that the mailing list that are made up from the poorest offers that resulted from the worst direct mail pieces for the worst ads, make the best mailing list. You know why? Because only the hungriest fish will snap at the crappiest bait. So a lot of times the list that will work best for you have been generated by some plotter who is going along on a slightly over break even basis. Anyway, indirect mail. If you read the SRDS book and start dialoguing with the list broker, you will have already captured, you will discover ponds that have bass and trout in them that haven't been fed per week. That's what you want more than anything. You know, you can have shrimp and I'll take an artificial lure and somebody else can fish with bacon and somebody else can fish with a night crawler. But what I want to do is I want to put my bait in the pond that has the most starving fish in it. Doesn't that just simply make sense? Now, let me tell you about newspapers, television, and other things. And it's not that they shouldn't be used. I don't mean that at all. But the most expensive thing in advertising, and this is what you really ought to write this down, it's only two words and those that have trouble seeing, I'll tell you what it is, it's waste circulation. Now, who reads the daily newspaper? Do any really elderly people read it? Do young kids read it? Yep, they do. Sometimes they just read the comics. People only interested in the sports pages read it. The people who are broke read the paper? Yep. People who are rich read the paper? Yep. People who use heroin read the paper? Drugs? Yes, they do. Every day. Do people who are terminally horned out read the paper? No. No. No. No. No. No. No. No. No. No. No. No. No. No. No. No. No. The point is that the paper is made up of a broad homogenous spectrum of readers. It. And when you are selling something in the paper, ask you to buy a page in that paper, they charge you so much for every 1,000 circulation of the paper. Now, you could say to them, what if you said to them, well, I only want the rich druggies who are horned out. They can't select for you. And so this is all you want. Let's suppose that that's only 3% of the circulation. Guess what? You have to pay for all of it. You have to pay for all of it. All that waste circulation. That same thing is true with radio. That same thing is true with television. Now, with radio and TV, it is somewhat more select, you know. It becomes a little more narrow cast. And in magazines, it becomes more narrow cast. But in direct mail, you don't have to present your lure to anything except trout and fish that haven't been fed for a couple of weeks. Do you all understand how important that is? So another big advantage of doing it in direct mail is you can literally find out the answer to the big question, usually, is this thing worth pursuing for less than $1,000? And you can find out very quickly and it's in your control. There's nobody censoring your direct mail. And in general, I just believe it is the very best way to go. Let's take the other end of the spectrum. What do I think is the worst thing you can do? No, I mean the worst way to sell. What do you think the worst way to sell it? Yeah, TV is the worst. I don't know what it is exactly, but Dan Kennedy says that about one out of every 14 shows make it and it's now up to something like the shows cost about $150,000 a piece to make. The infomercials, yeah. Those are real, real bad odds. How'd you like to spend $150,000 three hours later to find out it won't fly? And have worked on it for four months. And believe me, it's like walking through a field of molasses to put a TV show together. You know, getting the celebrities and getting everybody signed releases and dealing with all of the alphabet agencies. Plus everything you're doing is right there in front of the whole world. Guess what? If you repeat your show, it's going to be knocked off in a micro-second. And the big companies are so difficult to work with anymore. I won't even work with the Guthrie Rinker called me. You know, I did a lot of the first successful infomercials, you know, maybe a dozen and a half of them or something. And it used to be, you could just go into the studio, shoot three shows for Chump Change, run it out, test it, keep going with the ones that work. Well now, those shows have to have really high production values because they are done on. The networks, there's a lot of competition, et cetera, et cetera, and it has become very bureaucratic. Dan Kennedy, who consults for Guthrie Rinker, told me recently that in, they had a conference call with a bunch of people on it. It's about 45 minutes. What they achieve is all of them agreeing on a time to have a conference call. You know. And so, in general, I would say that you should stay away from that. Now, one of the things that helps make advertising work is if you really, really struggle to find unique ways to offer incredible value to your people. That's really a neat thing. Not just be selling hot air. And Sam has discovered a source of cameras. This is a picture of one of those cameras. He sent them to me. And I'll tell you, I am a camera buff. I'm loony tunes about cameras. That's my hobby. I now own, in fact, Alan has it in this room. The lens, there's probably only six other people in the United States have. But anyway, this camera has a swivel head flash. It's got a cord so that the flash is detachable. It's got a dual viewfinder, which means it's direct view. Or you can look down at it on reflex. I mean, it's just a superb camera. It looks like a professional camera costing at least five or six, seven hundred dollars. He's able to buy them for 25 bucks. Had him send me 10 of them. And so, these are a real value. And he gives them away. Sometimes you ask, you know, is this accurate? Sometimes we invent stories and then make them accurate. You know? And here's a story where we had to go damage one of these warehouses deliberately. Only this weekend, if you need new furniture, wait until you read this. Local furniture dealer is now giving away pre-professional cameras to women who can prove they live in Colorado. Please take a look at the camera that is in the picture on this page. This camera is amazing. It has a suggested retail price of three hundred and ninety-five dollars. There is no other camera like it in the entire world. It makes taking automatic professional quality photographs as easy as pushing a button. It has a 50 millimeter focus free lens that was made in Japan under conditions of very strict quality control. It has auto-wind and rewind, a dual viewfinder, a deluxe fast recycling electronic flash, a self timer, plus it comes with a custom design case and shoulder strap. Even though this camera takes professional quality photographs, anyone, even a young child, can learn how to use it in less than three minutes. This camera is very rare. You will never find anything like it in the world. However, if you are a woman who needs furniture and you can prove you live in the state of Colorado, you can have one of these cameras absolutely free. But you have to hurry. I have a fairly large supply of these cameras, but I have decided to get all of them away free before this weekend is over. Here's why. My name is Leslie Fishbine and I own all of the KC Fine Furniture Stores in the Denver area. Unfortunately, however, I have made a very serious mistake. You see, I have just received a ship in a brand new sofa, bedroom, and dining room set that I have to get rid of in just three days. This furniture is exclusive and incredibly beautiful, but I have had this furniture shipped to the wrong warehouse before my husband had finished getting the roof repaired. What this means is if it rains in the next few days, all of this inventory will become worthless. How serious am I about getting rid of all this furniture this weekend? Actually, I am so serious about it, I have decided to offer every woman who can prove she lives in the state of Colorado a quote unquote bribe no matter how much or how little. I don't care if it's as much as $10,000 or as little as $50 worth of furniture she decides to buy this weekend. The bribe is the camera I've been telling you about and is the one on the photograph on this page. Why am I willing to give away such a valuable camera to any Colorado woman that decides to buy furniture from me this weekend? The answer is easy. You see, I think there is a good chance that anyone who even looks at this collection of furniture, which I had shipped by mistake to the wrong warehouse, will see several items that will make her say, I've just got to have that sofa or bed or dining room set or whatever. Therefore, I have decided to do something very unusual to make sure all of this furniture gets sold during this coming weekend. If you need new furniture and you come to any of my stores this weekend only, here's the deal. I will get this. Yada yada yada yada. We closed it, it sounds like I'm crazy, doesn't it? Well, I'm not. All I am is a very motivated woman who has been forced to create the biggest furniture sale in my life because I have no other choice. Maybe I'm wrong, but maybe I'm not. In any case, I'm so desperate to get rid of all this furniture before the great ruins that I am willing to practice and give it away. Plus, I'll give you a free camera just for coming in and spending as little as $50 at any of my stores. Look, all I'm asking you to do is look, you don't have to buy anything. You will not even have to talk with a salesperson. There will be no pressure on you to buy anything. I simply don't allow stuff like that to go on in any of my stores. But the truth is, I am desperate to get you and other people like you to at least look at these amazing bargains in the hopes that you will want at least some of them. But I'll tell you what, I'll never make a mistake like this again, and I'm offering a special deal this weekend on Yada yada yada. I was talking to Sam, I said, you know, can we do this? And he said, Gary, I will go up on the roof with the sledgehammer, knock on the wall, sit on the roof, you know. So, you know, I could go on and on in this, but this will give you an idea of how two professionals who really know what they're doing can work with blinding speed and put this out. Can I just say knock on the roof? Yeah, you get all of them.
